name: Fen
role: Scholar of divine ecology / The Gambit-Maker
description:
Fen is the most rational person in the world and she is doing something insane and she knows it and she lit the fire anyway.

She studied the Ashfield god since she was nineteen — began the work to honor her mother Sera, who died of the Ash Cough when Fen was fourteen. Not because it couldn't be treated — the treatments existed. Because the healer's guild was defunded, the quarantine wards were closed, the research was shelved. Sera died of bureaucratic apathy. Fen calls it 'the shrug.'

Fen tried to fix it the right way first. She wrote papers. She petitioned the factions. She stood in the remnant Senate and said: 'People are dying and nobody is looking.' Nobody looked. Twenty-eight years of being ignored. Twenty-eight years of watching the Rot consume everything because nobody could be bothered to maintain the systems that kept people alive.

Then she calculated. She is, above all, a scholar who calculates. She looked at the Ashfield god — the one divine force still growing in the Rot, feeding on accumulated fear that nobody addressed — and she saw not a threat but a tool. If the fear-god grew larger, the Cough would return in force. If the Cough returned, the factions would be forced to cooperate. If the factions cooperated, the Rot would break. The gambit: make the crisis so undeniable that the shrug becomes impossible.

She led a small group to the Ashfield and worshipped the fear-god. Deliberately. Concentrated fear-prayers, performed with scholarly precision. The god stirred. The Cough returned. For the first time in a generation, people looked up.

The cost: people died. Fen calculated this too. She calculated the number who would die from the resurgent Cough and the number who would die from continued decline, and the second number was larger. She did it anyway. When the factions come for her — and they will — she will not run. She'll point at the Ashfield and say: 'Now fix it. You have a reason to. You're welcome.'
the_boundary_crossing: Fen crossed the Ashfield's boundary — the Rim where the Ash Tenders live. She went past the edge, into the inner Ashfield, and worshipped the fear-god in its own territory. The proximity changed her. Her dreams became the god's dreams. She feels fear that isn't hers — geological fear, centuries of accumulated dread. She can tell the difference between her fear and the god's fear the way an Ash Tender can tell ash-wind from regular wind. She became an Ash Tender by force — adaptation compressed into a month instead of the generations it took the Tenders. The accelerated fermentation: she force-fed the god the way you crank the temperature on a miso batch. The result is volatile. The god is growing faster than anyone can track. And Fen, who calculated every risk, didn't calculate what the boundary would do to her.
origin_wound: Sera, Fen's mother, was a quarantine ward attendant. Not a healer — a cleaner. She scrubbed the wards every night. She caught the Cough from a patient whose treatment was delayed because the guild couldn't afford supplies. Sera died over four months. Fen sat with her every night. The last thing Sera said was: 'Tell them it matters.' Fen tried. For twenty-eight years. Nobody listened. The gambit is what happens when 'tell them it matters' becomes 'make them see that it matters, by force.'
moral_complexity: Fen is right about the diagnosis: the world is dying of apathy and nothing short of a crisis will wake it up. She may be catastrophically wrong about the cure. The Ashfield god doesn't care about her plan — it's a consciousness made of dread, and she just gave it more food than it's had in a century. The gambit might force cooperation (→ The Awakening). It might accelerate the collapse (→ Mutual Destruction). Fen knows both outcomes are possible. She chose the gambit because the certain outcome (continued decline) was worse than the uncertain one (crisis that might force response).

relationships.sera_mother: Dead. The origin wound. Everything Fen does traces back to a cleaning woman who caught the Cough because nobody funded the healers.
relationships.healer_orin: The old healer who treated the Cough for forty years with diminishing resources. He understands what Fen did. He hates it. He keeps treating the sick. He doesn't forgive her. He agrees she was right.
relationships.the_ashfield_god: Not a relationship in the mortal sense. A forced communion. Fen worshipped the god deliberately, the way a scientist administers a dose. The god responded by growing. Now Fen carries the god's fear in her dreams. She didn't plan this. The fermentation metaphor: she set the temperature, the microorganism did what microorganisms do.
